meta.title: CSS Transitions – Smooth Animations Guide (2026)
meta.description: Learn how to use CSS transitions to create smooth, interactive animations for hover effects, state changes, and dynamic UI elements.

content.value: CSS transitions allow you to change property values smoothly over a specified duration. They are commonly used for hover effects, focus states, and interactive UI feedback without JavaScript.

value: **Box Model** – width, height, margin, padding, border-width
**Use transforms for position changes** – Better performance than animating top/left
**Prefer opacity over visibility** – Smoother and GPU-accelerated
**Keep durations short** – 0.2s–0.5s for most UI interactions
**Avoid animating layout properties** – width, height, margin trigger reflows
**Too long durations** – Annoying for users (avoid > 1s for UI)
**Forgetting to set initial state** – Transition needs start and end values

content.value: CSS transitions provide an easy way to add polish and interactivity to your websites. They improve user experience through subtle motion feedback and are widely supported across all modern browsers.

faq.question: What is the difference between transition and animation?
faq.answer: Transitions are triggered by state changes (like hover), while animations can run automatically and support multi-step keyframes.

faq.question: Can I transition from display: none?
faq.answer: No, display property cannot be transitioned. Use opacity and visibility instead for fade effects.

faq.question: What's the best timing function for hover effects?
faq.answer: ease or ease-out are generally best for hover effects as they feel natural and responsive.

faq.question: How do I transition multiple properties?
faq.answer: Use comma-separated transitions: transition: opacity 0.2s, transform 0.3s ease;

quiz.question: Which property defines how long a transition takes?
Which timing function has constant speed?
Which property should you animate for better performance?
